description.narrative: The overall objective of the CGV programme is “Sustainable conservation of the Greater Virunga Landscape, through transboundary collaboration between the Democratic Republic of Congo, Rwanda and Uganda”. The overall objective is derived from the mandate of GVTC as given in the Treaty and reflects the GVTC Vision “The Greater Virunga Landscape sustainably conserved” and Mission “Enhancing sustainable conservation of the Protected Areas in Greater Virunga Landscape through improved Transboundary collaboration” as described in the Transboundary Strategic Plan (TSP) 2018-2023.

In November 2020, the Minister of Foreign Trade and Development Cooperation of the Kingdom of the Netherlands and the Greater Virunga Transboundary Collaboration (GVTC) formalised a cooperation arrangement. This partnership, supported through the Embassy of the Kingdom of the Netherlands in Kigali, funds the "Conserving the Greater Virunga Landscape (CGV)" project. The overarching objective of the CGV project is to ensure the sustainable conservation of the Greater Virunga Landscape (GVL) through transboundary collaboration between the Democratic Republic of Congo, Rwanda, and Uganda. Three key intermediary objectives drive the project: 1. Intermediary Objective 1: Coordinate and promote the conservation of biodiversity in the GVL. 2. Intermediary Objective 2: Foster collaboration and coordination of natural resource-based socio-economic development in the GVL. 3. Intermediary Objective 3: Establish conditions for sustainable transboundary collaboration on conservation and tourism. As the four-year project is ending in December 2024, GVTC has prioritised activities this final year to ensure the organisation's sustainability. Key activities planned include coordinating meetings with protected area (PA) staff, establishing a climate change monitoring framework for the GVL (with a focus on forest monitoring), developing GVL as a unified destination brand across all partner states (in collaboration with the EAC Secretariat), and enhancing local communities' capacity in tourism business development. Additionally, efforts to promote collaboration and coordination include fast-tracking and lobbying partner states for the ratification of the GVTC Treaty and conducting a country-level stakeholder meeting in Rwanda. To stimulate GVTC's sustainability, branding initiatives, publicising accomplishments, participating in key regional symposiums, workshops, and world conservation events, publishing articles and data on GVL, and securing new funding sources were identified as crucial activities. Further, the project is focused on coordinating preparations for the 2024/2025 gorilla census, developing the Transboundary Strategic Plan (TSP), supporting gender mainstreaming in conservation, holding statutory meetings such as the GVTC Management Board, Council of Ministers, Regional Technical Committees (RTCs), and regional stakeholders’ forums, and building the capacity of GVTC staff to fulfil the organisation’s mandate. This mid-year report has been prepared to document and demonstrate the progress made in implementing the planned activities. It outlines the project context in light of the evolving political landscape in the region, updates the risk assessment, details the implemented activities, and provides an overview of the annual implementation status.

The management of protected areas network of the Greater Virunga Landscape: Volcanoes NP in Rwanda; Virunga NP & Sarambwe Wildlife Reserve in DRC and Mgahinga Gorilla NP, Queen Elizabeth NP, Bwindi NP, Rwenzori Mountain NP, and Semliki NP in Uganda; The communities in the Greater Virunga landscape and The GVTC conservation partners organisations operating in Greater Virunga Landscape

participating-org.narrative: In November 2020, the Minister of Foreign Trade and Development Cooperation of the Kingdom of the Netherlands and the Greater Virunga Transboundary Collaboration (GVTC) formalised a cooperation arrangement. This partnership, supported through the Embassy of the Kingdom of the Netherlands in Kigali, funds the "Conserving the Greater Virunga Landscape (CGV)" project. The overarching objective of the CGV project is to ensure the sustainable conservation of the Greater Virunga Landscape (GVL) through transboundary collaboration between the Democratic Republic of Congo, Rwanda, and Uganda.

In November 2020, the Minister of Foreign Trade and Development Cooperation of the Kingdom of the Netherlands and the Greater Virunga Transboundary Collaboration (GVTC) formalised a cooperation arrangement. This partnership, supported through the Embassy of the Kingdom of the Netherlands in Kigali, funds the "Conserving the Greater Virunga Landscape (CGV)" project. The overarching objective of the CGV project is to ensure the sustainable conservation of the Greater Virunga Landscape (GVL) through transboundary collaboration between the Democratic Republic of Congo, Rwanda, and Uganda.

The three Governments signed a Transboundary Collaboration Treaty and created an Executive Secretariat to manage day to day activities of the collaboration to conserve the shared resources within the Greater Virunga Landscape

owner-org.narrative:
This is the progressive narrative report for Conserving Greater Virunga for the period from January to June 2024.
The overall objective of the CGV programme is “Sustainable conservation of the Greater Virunga Landscape, through transboundary collaboration between the Democratic Republic of Congo, Rwanda and Uganda”. The overall objective is derived from the mandate of GVTC as given in the Treaty and reflects the GVTC Vision “The Greater Virunga Landscape sustainably conserved” and Mission “Enhancing sustainable conservation of the Protected Areas in Greater Virunga Landscape through improved Transboundary collaboration” as described in the Transboundary Strategic Plan (TSP) 2018-2023.
There are three intermediary objectives that the programme aims to achieve: Intermediary Objective 1: Coordinate and promote conservation of biodiversity within the GVL Intermediary Objective 2: Promote collaboration and coordination of Natural resource based socio-economic development in the GVL Intermediary Objective 3: Create conditions for sustainable transboundary collaboration on conservation and tourism
The management of protected areas listed in appendix 1 of GVTC treaty namely Volcanoes NP in Rwanda; Virunga NP & Sarambwe Wildlife Reserve in DRC and Mgahinga Gorilla NP, Queen Elizabeth NP, Bwindi NP, Rwenzori Mountain NP, and Semliki NP in Uganda; The communities in the Greater Virunga landscape and The GVTC conservation partners organisations operating in Greater Virunga Landscape

activity-date.narrative: As the four-year project is ending in December 2024, GVTC has prioritised activities this final year to ensure the organisation's sustainability. Key activities planned include coordinating meetings with protected area (PA) staff, establishing a climate change monitoring framework for the GVL (with a focus on forest monitoring), developing GVL as a unified destination brand across all partner states (in collaboration with the EAC Secretariat), and enhancing local communities' capacity in tourism business development. Additionally, efforts to promote collaboration and coordination include fast-tracking and lobbying partner states for the ratification of the GVTC Treaty and conducting a country-level stakeholder meeting in Rwanda. To stimulate GVTC's sustainability, branding initiatives, publicising accomplishments, participating in key regional symposiums, workshops, and world conservation events, publishing articles and data on GVL, and securing new funding sources were identified as crucial activities. Further, the project is focused on coordinating preparations for the 2024/2025 gorilla census, developing the Transboundary Strategic Plan (TSP), supporting gender mainstreaming in conservation, holding statutory meetings such as the GVTC Management Board, Council of Ministers, Regional Technical Committees (RTCs), and regional stakeholders’ forums, and building the capacity of GVTC staff to fulfil the organisation’s mandate.

This mid-year report has been prepared to document and demonstrate the progress made in implementing the planned activities. It outlines the project context in light of the evolving political landscape in the region, updates the risk assessment, details the implemented activities, and provides an overview of the annual implementation status.

Establishing a Monitoring Framework for Climate Change Effects on the GVL The Greater Virunga Transboundary Collaboration (GVTC) has partnered with SarVision to develop a forest monitoring system using SarSentry, a sentinel-1 radar technology developed at Wageningen University in the Netherlands. This system aims to support the monitoring of changes and disturbances in the forests of the Greater Virunga Landscape (GVL). The partnership focuses on building capacity and transferring technology for advanced and automated real-time monitoring, which includes reporting forest disturbances, land use, and livelihood changes. These factors are critical in understanding the impacts of climate change and improving law enforcement efforts. In the first half of the year, GVTC signed a contract with SarVision to develop this system. So far, real-time maps have been developed, and a dedicated forest viewer has been set up. The current version is already online (https://app.sarvision.nl/virunga/); it has a language selector (English, French, Kinyarwanda and Kiswahili) and it has an online manual. In the second half of the year, SarVision will implement an alerting system, incorporate site-specific information, and train protected area staff on how to use the system. The monitoring framework is expected to be fully rolled out and transferred to protected area management by November 2024.

Enhancing Local Communities' Capacity in Tourism Business GVTC conducted consultations with various stakeholders, including tourists, guides, trackers, porters, craft sellers, and protected area management teams in Bwindi Impenetrable National Park, Mgahinga Gorilla National Park, and Volcanoes National Park. These consultations revealed significant challenges, such as the lack of unified platforms to secure tour services like guides and porters. Local service providers struggle with visibility and connecting with potential tourists, leading to missed opportunities. To address these challenges, GVTC has designed an intervention to enhance communities' capacity and benefits from tourism by providing a digital platform. The proposed platform, available as a web-based and Android application, will enable tourists to find and pay for essential services such as guides and porters while offering local craft sellers a digital marketplace to showcase their products. A consulting firm has already begun developing the platform, which will serve as a centralised hub for tourists to plan their expeditions and support the local economy. The platform will be rolled out, and users in the three pilot-protected areas will be trained in October 2024. GVTC will continue to monitor and provide technical support throughout 2025 before handing the platform over to the authorities.
In addition, GVTC has engaged the East African Community Secretariat. A meeting with the EAC Secretary General and his team was requested, and it is expected to take place in October 2024 at the EAC office in Tanzania. This meeting aims to benchmark the EAC policy on tourism in the Greater Virunga Landscape shared between the three countries that are, at the same time, members of EAC.

Participation and Presentation at Key Regional Symposiums and Workshops In March 2024, GVTC participated in the African Evaluation Association (AfrEA) conference held in Kigali, Rwanda. The delegation included two representatives from the GVTC Executive Secretariat and a Protected Area. During the conference, GVTC presented a paper on utilising monitoring and evaluation information in community-based natural resources management. Several international non-governmental organisations, foundations like MasterCard, universities, and other stakeholders attended the conference. This participation provided GVTC with the opportunity to network and increase its visibility in the conservation community.

Coordinating Joint Censuses for Gorillas and Savanna Big Mammals The gorilla census for Bwindi-Sarambwe is scheduled to begin in October and conclude in March 2025. GVTC's role in this process is to coordinate state and non-state partners to collaborate and contribute both human and financial resources. In collaboration with the International Gorilla Conservation Programme (IGCP), which is leading the technical component, GVTC held planning meetings to determine resource requirements, roles, and contributing partners. During the reporting period, GVTC also mobilised protected area authorities to identify and plan for the human resources needed for the census, enhancing their participation and ownership of the process. A Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) for collaboration has been drafted and will be discussed with identified contributors in August 2024, alongside other coordination and planning activities. Following this, the team plans to start the census in the Virunga Massif, provided the security situation allows.

Publishing Articles and Data on the GVL GVTC has revitalised a technical working group to prepare the Greater Virunga Landscape (GVL) Annual Conservation Status Report (ACSR) and has mobilised partners to contribute data and participate in writing the report. GVTC is undertaking a critical coordination role in the region. The partners, consisting of conservation NGOs from the three countries implementing programs in the GVL, will collaborate through a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) in data collection, sharing, and reporting. In the second half of the year, GVTC, along with its partners, will develop a framework for and write the ACSR for 2024. GVTC website is updated regularly, and articles on its achievements are posted on a regular basis. This allows all the partners to follow updates on GVTC and increases visibility, transparency, and accountability. GVTC has started producing the documentary film. The documentary will be served during the exhibition at international conferences to raise awareness of the initiative of the Transboundary Collaboration while stimulating support for the GVTC cause.

Development of the Transboundary Strategic Plan The Transboundary Strategic Plan (TSP) for the GVL was developed within the GVTC framework through a collaborative approach. The WWF Uganda Country Office funded a consultant to facilitate the process and draft the TSP. At the same time, GVTC, with funding from the Embassy of the Kingdom of the Netherlands (EKN), covered the costs of consultations and validation meetings. The TSP was finalised in April 2024, and a Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability, and Learning (MEAL) plan to guide performance measurement was developed. Additionally, a one-year action plan was created to kick-start the implementation of the TSP. While GVTC has successfully mobilised collaboration and is actively coordinating state and non-state agencies to implement the GVL TSP, a significant challenge moving forward is securing the necessary resources to finance these efforts.

Securing New Sources of Funding Ensuring the sustainability of GVTC has become a critical objective in 2024. So far, two proposals have been submitted to various funding sources: the United Kingdom Challenge Fund on Illegal Wildlife Trade (IWT) for the GVL Zero Poaching Strategy and the World Bank for pandemic preparedness in the GVL. The IWT project proposal, written in collaboration with WWF, WCS, IGCP, and others, was approved, and implementation will commence in August 2024. The outcome of the pandemic fund application is expected before the year ends. Additionally, two concept notes have been submitted, including one to the European Union Delegation to Rwanda on promoting collaboration and coordination in natural resources management in the GVL and the UK Biodiversity Challenge Fund/Darwin Initiative on invasive species management in Uganda and DRC in partnership with the International Institute for Sustainable Development (IISD). To further support these efforts, a Technical Advisor has been hired to lead fundraising initiatives. The advisor has already convened meetings with USAID Uganda and the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)/United States Forest Service (USFS) to discuss potential funding opportunities. In the second half of the year, the focus will be on following up on these engagements, reviewing and updating the current fundraising strategy, and implementing action plans to secure funding for GVTC's sustainability.

mailing-address.narrative: Rwanda- Kigali- Gasabo KG9 Av. N°1A P.O.BOX 6626 KIGALI

recipient-country.narrative:
GVTC is an Intergovernmental Organization established by the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Republic of Rwanda and the Republic of Uganda governed by a Treaty signed in 2015, whose purpose is to establish a Transboundary Collaboration Framework for programs and activities on wildlife conservation and tourism development in the Greater Virunga Landscape among the Partner States.
This Landscape comprises a total of 7 national parks and 1 wildlife reserve, namely: Volcanoes NP in Rwanda; Virunga NP & Sarambwe Wildlife Reserve in DRC and Mgahinga Gorilla NP, Queen Elizabeth NP, Bwindi NP, Rwenzori Mountain NP, and Semliki NP in Uganda. The GVL is globally recognized as a biodiversity hotspot and habitat for rare and endangered wildlife species, such as mountain gorillas, chimpanzees, African Elephants, Lions, hippopotamus extra with three World Heritage Sites (the Virunga NP, Rwenzori Mountains, and Bwindi NP), two Man & Biosphere Reserves (MAB, namely the Queen Elizabeth NP and Volcanoes NP), as well as RAMSAR sites (Lakes Georges and Edward).
GVTC then operates equally in the three partner states, Democratic Republic of Congo, Republic of Rwanda and the Republic of Uganda.
GVTC conducts the Conserving Greater Virunga activities in the three GVTC partner states: Democratic Republic of Congo, Republic of Rwanda and the Republic of Uganda.
GVTC conducts the Conserving Greater Virunga activities in the three GVTC partner states: Democratic Republic of Congo, Republic of Rwanda and the Republic of Uganda.

recipient-region.narrative: CGV Programme is implemented in the Greater Virunga Landscape shared between the Greater Virunga Transboundary Collaboration Partner States, namely the Democratic Republic of Congo, the Republic of Rwanda and the Republic of Uganda

sector.narrative: GVTC is an Intergovernmental Organization established by the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Republic of Rwanda and the Republic of Uganda governed by a Treaty signed in 2015, whose purpose is to establish a Transboundary Collaboration Framework for programs and activities on wildlife conservation and tourism development in the Greater Virunga Landscape among the Partner States.

Biodiversity Conservation and Tourism Development through Transboundary Collaboration. GVTC is an Intergovernmental Organization established by the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), Republic of Rwanda and the Republic of Uganda governed by a Treaty signed in 2015, whose purpose is to establish a Transboundary Collaboration Framework for programs and activities on wildlife conservation and tourism development in the Greater Virunga Landscape among the Partner States.

tag.narrative: Conserving Greater Virunga Landscape and Promote Tourism Development
